meta.title: SG-SCD-4: Workbooks, Modeling, and Embedded Delivery

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. A workbook needs one selector to drive several charts at the same time. Which Sigma feature should the developer configure?

options.text: A control with targets mapped to the relevant elements
A warehouse role assigned to each chart
A materialization schedule on every worksheet
A JWT embed claim that rewrites filter logic

questions.explanation: A control with targets is correct because Sigma controls can drive one or more workbook elements for coordinated filtering. Warehouse roles, materialization schedules, and embed claims solve different problems.

questions.stem: A team is designing a new solution. A large join runs before filters and the workbook becomes slow. What is the best first optimization?

options.text: Duplicate the workbook into another folder
Remove all controls from the page
Switch every table to manual refresh
Push selective filters earlier so fewer rows enter the join

questions.explanation: Applying selective filters earlier is correct because reducing join input volume is a primary way to lower query cost and latency. The other choices do not address the root query-shape problem.

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. An application wants embedded Sigma analytics for signed-in users. What should the host application generate server-side?

options.text: A copy of all workbook data in the app database
A browser extension that bypasses Sigma authentication
A signed embed URL or JWT with the required claims
A static HTML export of the workbook

questions.explanation: A signed embed URL or JWT is correct because Sigma embedding relies on server-generated trusted context. Static exports, data copying, and authentication bypasses are not the intended pattern.

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. A company wants least-privilege access to analytics content. Which sharing approach is safest?

options.text: Replace permissions with input table writeback rules
Grant access only to the specific folders or workbooks each audience needs
Make all workspaces editable by every viewer
Turn off authentication for internal users

questions.explanation: Granular sharing is correct because it limits access to only the required content. Broad edit access, disabled authentication, and writeback settings do not provide least-privilege content control.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. A Sigma developer wants reusable curated logic for many workbooks. What should they publish?

options.text: A dataset that centralizes the modeled logic
A screenshot of the current workbook
A browser bookmark collection
A personal note attached to a chart

questions.explanation: A dataset is correct because it lets teams publish reusable modeled data for many workbooks. Screenshots, bookmarks, and notes do not create governed reusable logic.

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. A planning workflow needs business users to enter assumptions directly in Sigma. Which feature supports that requirement?

options.text: A context filter
A dashboard subscription only
A warehouse resize policy
An input table

questions.explanation: An input table is correct because Sigma input tables are designed for user-entered data and planning-style workflows. Filters, subscriptions, and warehouse sizing do not capture governed entered values.

questions.stem: A team is designing a new solution. A workbook runs the same expensive logic repeatedly for many viewers. What Sigma feature can improve performance by persisting results?

options.text: Replacing all filters with text boxes
Disabling dataset governance
Materialization of the expensive result set
Adding more worksheets to the workbook

questions.explanation: Materialization is correct because it can persist expensive query results for faster reuse. The other choices either increase complexity or weaken governance without solving repeated compute.

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. A developer considers using a cross join between two large tables. Why is that risky?

options.text: It prevents users from exporting CSV results
It can create a Cartesian product that dramatically increases rows and query cost
It automatically disables workbook controls
It permanently deletes the source dataset

questions.explanation: A Cartesian-product explosion is correct because cross joins multiply row counts and often create expensive warehouse work. Controls, source deletion, and export behavior are unrelated.

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. A KPI should show the exact value when a user hovers over a mark without changing the query grain. Which workbook element behavior is most relevant?

options.text: Tooltips on the visualization
JWT team claims
Workbook duplication
Warehouse failover

questions.explanation: Tooltips are correct because they provide additional context on hover without changing the displayed grain. JWT claims, duplication, and warehouse failover do not address chart hover details.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. A product team wants embedded users to inherit region-based row restrictions from the application context. What embed design is appropriate?

options.text: Export all data and filter it in JavaScript only
Share one generic admin account with every customer
Disable Sigma authentication checks
Pass trusted user attributes or claims in the embed configuration

questions.explanation: Trusted user attributes or claims are correct because embedded experiences commonly personalize access with signed context. Client-side-only filtering, shared admin accounts, and disabled authentication are insecure.

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. A developer can open a workbook but cannot change its dataset. What permission gap is the most likely cause?

options.text: Input tables always override object permissions
Workbook controls prevent dataset editing
They have view access but not edit permission on the relevant content or source object
Sigma requires all viewers to be organization admins

questions.explanation: A view-versus-edit permission gap is correct because Sigma permissions can allow access without granting modification rights. Organization-admin requirements, input tables, and controls are not the cause.

questions.stem: A team is designing a new solution. Two datasets must be combined while preserving only matching business keys. Which join type is usually appropriate?

options.text: No modeling step at all
Inner join
Cross join
Full duplication without a join

questions.explanation: An inner join is correct because it retains rows that match on the join key in both tables. Cross joins, duplication, and skipping modeling do not satisfy the matching-keys requirement.

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. A calculation should reuse logic consistently across many workbook elements. What is generally preferable?

options.text: Centralize the logic in a reusable dataset or governed calculation
Rewrite the formula differently in every chart
Ask each viewer to calculate the metric manually
Replace the metric with free-text comments

questions.explanation: Centralizing reusable logic is correct because it reduces drift and keeps metrics consistent. Rewriting formulas everywhere, relying on viewers, or replacing metrics with text increases inconsistency.

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. A team wants users to adjust assumptions but still audit who changed what. Why are governed input-table workflows useful?

options.text: They automatically bypass warehouse permissions
They eliminate the need for any data model
They convert every value into a visualization
They support structured data entry within a governed workflow

questions.explanation: Structured governed data entry is correct because input tables support controlled user submissions in the analytics workflow. They do not bypass permissions, remove modeling, or turn all values into charts.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. A metric should be instantly understandable on a landing page with minimal detail. Which visual is usually the best first choice?

options.text: An embed token editor
A warehouse permission matrix
A KPI or single-value visualization
A cross join configuration table

questions.explanation: A KPI-style single-value visual is correct because it communicates one important metric quickly. The other options are not designed to present a simple headline measure.

questions.stem: A team is designing a new solution. Users must change one date range and have multiple elements respond together. Which feature handles that shared interaction?

options.text: A control with targets mapped to the relevant elements
A warehouse role assigned to each chart
A materialization schedule on every worksheet
A JWT embed claim that rewrites filter logic

questions.explanation: A control with targets is correct because Sigma controls can drive one or more workbook elements for coordinated filtering. Warehouse roles, materialization schedules, and embed claims solve different problems.

questions.explanation: Applying selective filters earlier is correct because reducing join input volume is a primary way to lower query cost and latency. The other choices do not address the root query-shape problem.
